question:
B-6. On the domain \( 0 \leqq \theta \leqq 2 \pi \) :
(a) Prove that \( \sin ^{2} \theta \cdot \sin (2 \theta) \) takes its maximum at \( \pi / 3 \) and \( 4 \pi / 3 \) (and hence its minimum at \( 2 \pi / 3 \) and \( 5 \pi / 3 \) ).
(b) Show that
\[
\left|\sin ^{2} \theta\left\{\sin ^{3}(2 \theta) \cdot \sin 3(4 \theta) \cdots \sin ^{3}\left(2^{n-1} \theta\right)\right\} \sin \left(2^{n} \theta\right)\right|
\]
takes its maximum at \( \theta=\pi / 3 \). (The maximum may also be attained at other points.)
(c) Derive the inequality:
\[
\sin ^{2} \theta \cdot \sin ^{2}(2 \theta) \cdot \sin ^{2}(4 \theta) \cdots \sin ^{2}\left(2^{n} \theta\right) \leqq(3 / 4)^{n}
\]
solution:
B-6. (a) Simple calculus.
(b) By induction: The case \( n=1 \) is just (a).

Now the ratio of the expression for \( n+1 \) to the expression for \( n \) is equal to:
\[
\left|\sin ^{2} 2^{n} \theta \cdot \sin 2^{n+1} \theta\right|
\]

Since \( \theta=\pi / 3 \) gives \( 2^{n} \theta \equiv 2 \pi / 3 \) or \( 4 \pi / 3(\bmod 2 \pi) \), this ratio is maximized at \( \theta=\pi / 3 \), and by induction, then, the whole expression is maximized.
(c) Set \( \theta=\pi / 3 \), and observe that the expression in part (b) is then exactly equal to \( (3 / 4)^{3 n / 2} \); its \( 2 / 3 \) power is thus equal to \( (3 / 4)^{n} \). That is the maximum; in general the \( 2 / 3 \) power of the expression in (b) is \( \leqq(3 / 4)^{n} \). To get from that to the expression in (c), we would increase the powers of the end factors \( \sin \theta \) and \( \sin 2^{n} \theta \); this can only decrease the product, since \( |\sin \theta| \leqq 1 \).

descriptive_long.question:
B-6. On the domain \( 0 \leqq angletheta \leqq 2 \pi \) :
(a) Prove that \( \sin ^{2} angletheta \cdot \sin (2 angletheta) \) takes its maximum at \( \pi / 3 \) and \( 4 \pi / 3 \) (and hence its minimum at \( 2 \pi / 3 \) and \( 5 \pi / 3 \) ).
(b) Show that
\[
\left|\sin ^{2} angletheta\left\{\sin ^{3}(2 angletheta) \cdot \sin 3(4 angletheta) \cdots \sin ^{3}\left(2^{indexcount-1} angletheta\right)\right\} \sin \left(2^{indexcount} angletheta\right)\right|
\]
takes its maximum at \( angletheta=\pi / 3 \). (The maximum may also be attained at other points.)
(c) Derive the inequality:
\[
\sin ^{2} angletheta \cdot \sin ^{2}(2 angletheta) \cdot \sin ^{2}(4 angletheta) \cdots \sin ^{2}\left(2^{indexcount} angletheta\right) \leqq(3 / 4)^{indexcount}
\]
descriptive_long.solution:
B-6. (a) Simple calculus.
(b) By induction: The case \( indexcount=1 \) is just (a).

Now the ratio of the expression for \( indexcount+1 \) to the expression for \( indexcount \) is equal to:
\[
\left|\sin ^{2} 2^{indexcount} angletheta \cdot \sin 2^{indexcount+1} angletheta\right|
\]

Since \( angletheta=\pi / 3 \) gives \( 2^{indexcount} angletheta \equiv 2 \pi / 3 \) or \( 4 \pi / 3(\bmod 2 \pi) \), this ratio is maximized at \( angletheta=\pi / 3 \), and by induction, then, the whole expression is maximized.
(c) Set \( angletheta=\pi / 3 \), and observe that the expression in part (b) is then exactly equal to \( (3 / 4)^{3 indexcount / 2} \); its \( 2 / 3 \) power is thus equal to \( (3 / 4)^{indexcount} \). That is the maximum; in general the \( 2 / 3 \) power of the expression in (b) is \( \leqq(3 / 4)^{indexcount} \). To get from that to the expression in (c), we would increase the powers of the end factors \( \sin angletheta \) and \( \sin 2^{indexcount} angletheta \); this can only decrease the product, since \( |\sin angletheta| \leqq 1 \).

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
B-6. On the domain \( 0 \leqq buttercup \leqq 2 \pi \) :
(a) Prove that \( \sin ^{2} buttercup \cdot \sin (2 buttercup) \) takes its maximum at \( \pi / 3 \) and \( 4 \pi / 3 \) (and hence its minimum at \( 2 \pi / 3 \) and \( 5 \pi / 3 \) ).
(b) Show that
\[
\left|\sin ^{2} buttercup\left\{\sin ^{3}(2 buttercup) \cdot \sin 3(4 buttercup) \cdots \sin ^{3}\left(2^{caterpillar-1} buttercup\right)\right\} \sin \left(2^{caterpillar} buttercup\right)\right|
\]
takes its maximum at \( buttercup=\pi / 3 \). (The maximum may also be attained at other points.)
(c) Derive the inequality:
\[
\sin ^{2} buttercup \cdot \sin ^{2}(2 buttercup) \cdot \sin ^{2}(4 buttercup) \cdots \sin ^{2}\left(2^{caterpillar} buttercup\right) \leqq(3 / 4)^{caterpillar}
\]
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
B-6. (a) Simple calculus.
(b) By induction: The case \( caterpillar=1 \) is just (a).

Now the ratio of the expression for \( caterpillar+1 \) to the expression for \( caterpillar \) is equal to:
\[
\left|\sin ^{2} 2^{caterpillar} buttercup \cdot \sin 2^{caterpillar+1} buttercup\right|
\]

Since \( buttercup=\pi / 3 \) gives \( 2^{caterpillar} buttercup \equiv 2 \pi / 3 \) or \( 4 \pi / 3(\bmod 2 \pi) \), this ratio is maximized at \( buttercup=\pi / 3 \), and by induction, then, the whole expression is maximized.
(c) Set \( buttercup=\pi / 3 \), and observe that the expression in part (b) is then exactly equal to \( (3 / 4)^{3 caterpillar / 2} \); its \( 2 / 3 \) power is thus equal to \( (3 / 4)^{caterpillar} \). That is the maximum; in general the \( 2 / 3 \) power of the expression in (b) is \( \leqq(3 / 4)^{caterpillar} \). To get from that to the expression in (c), we would increase the powers of the end factors \( \sin buttercup \) and \( \sin 2^{caterpillar} buttercup \); this can only decrease the product, since \( |\sin buttercup| \leqq 1 \).

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
B-6. On the domain \( 0 \leqq straightline \leqq 2 \pi \) :
(a) Prove that \( \sin ^{2} straightline \cdot \sin (2 straightline) \) takes its maximum at \( \pi / 3 \) and \( 4 \pi / 3 \) (and hence its minimum at \( 2 \pi / 3 \) and \( 5 \pi / 3 \) ).
(b) Show that
\[
\left|\sin ^{2} straightline\left\{\sin ^{3}(2 straightline) \cdot \sin 3(4 straightline) \cdots \sin ^{3}\left(2^{uncountable-1} straightline\right)\right\} \sin \left(2^{uncountable} straightline\right)\right|
\]
 takes its maximum at \( straightline=\pi / 3 \). (The maximum may also be attained at other points.)
(c) Derive the inequality:
\[
\sin ^{2} straightline \cdot \sin ^{2}(2 straightline) \cdot \sin ^{2}(4 straightline) \cdots \sin ^{2}\left(2^{uncountable} straightline\right) \leqq(3 / 4)^{uncountable}
\]
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
B-6. (a) Simple calculus.
(b) By induction: The case \( uncountable=1 \) is just (a).

Now the ratio of the expression for \( uncountable+1 \) to the expression for \( uncountable \) is equal to:
\[
\left|\sin ^{2} 2^{uncountable} straightline \cdot \sin 2^{uncountable+1} straightline\right|
\]

Since \( straightline=\pi / 3 \) gives \( 2^{uncountable} straightline \equiv 2 \pi / 3 \) or \( 4 \pi / 3(\bmod 2 \pi) \), this ratio is maximized at \( straightline=\pi / 3 \), and by induction, then, the whole expression is maximized.
(c) Set \( straightline=\pi / 3 \), and observe that the expression in part (b) is then exactly equal to \( (3 / 4)^{3 uncountable / 2} \); its \( 2 / 3 \) power is thus equal to \( (3 / 4)^{uncountable} \). That is the maximum; in general the \( 2 / 3 \) power of the expression in (b) is \( \leqq(3 / 4)^{uncountable} \). To get from that to the expression in (c), we would increase the powers of the end factors \( \sin straightline \) and \( \sin 2^{uncountable} straightline \); this can only decrease the product, since \( |\sin straightline| \leqq 1 \).

garbled_string.question:
B-6. On the domain \( 0 \leqq qzxwvtnp \leqq 2 \pi \) :
(a) Prove that \( \sin ^{2} qzxwvtnp \cdot \sin (2 qzxwvtnp) \) takes its maximum at \( \pi / 3 \) and \( 4 \pi / 3 \) (and hence its minimum at \( 2 \pi / 3 \) and \( 5 \pi / 3 \) ).
(b) Show that
\[
\left|\sin ^{2} qzxwvtnp\left\{\sin ^{3}(2 qzxwvtnp) \cdot \sin 3(4 qzxwvtnp) \cdots \sin ^{3}\left(2^{hjgrksla-1} qzxwvtnp\right)\right\} \sin \left(2^{hjgrksla} qzxwvtnp\right)\right|
\]
 takes its maximum at \( qzxwvtnp=\pi / 3 \). (The maximum may also be attained at other points.)
(c) Derive the inequality:
\[
\sin ^{2} qzxwvtnp \cdot \sin ^{2}(2 qzxwvtnp) \cdot \sin ^{2}(4 qzxwvtnp) \cdots \sin ^{2}\left(2^{hjgrksla} qzxwvtnp\right) \leqq(3 / 4)^{hjgrksla}
\]
garbled_string.solution:
B-6. (a) Simple calculus.
(b) By induction: The case \( hjgrksla=1 \) is just (a).

Now the ratio of the expression for \( hjgrksla+1 \) to the expression for \( hjgrksla \) is equal to:
\[
\left|\sin ^{2} 2^{hjgrksla} qzxwvtnp \cdot \sin 2^{hjgrksla+1} qzxwvtnp\right|
\]

Since \( qzxwvtnp=\pi / 3 \) gives \( 2^{hjgrksla} qzxwvtnp \equiv 2 \pi / 3 \) or \( 4 \pi / 3(\bmod 2 \pi) \), this ratio is maximized at \( qzxwvtnp=\pi / 3 \), and by induction, then, the whole expression is maximized.
(c) Set \( qzxwvtnp=\pi / 3 \), and observe that the expression in part (b) is then exactly equal to \( (3 / 4)^{3 hjgrksla / 2} \); its \( 2 / 3 \) power is thus equal to \( (3 / 4)^{hjgrksla} \). That is the maximum; in general the \( 2 / 3 \) power of the expression in (b) is \( \leqq(3 / 4)^{hjgrksla} \). To get from that to the expression in (c), we would increase the powers of the end factors \( \sin qzxwvtnp \) and \( \sin 2^{hjgrksla} qzxwvtnp \); this can only decrease the product, since \( |\sin qzxwvtnp| \leqq 1 \).

kernel_variant.question:
Let n be a non-negative integer and let 0\le \theta\le 2\pi.  Define

P_{n}(\theta)=\;\sin ^{2}\!\theta\;\Bigl[\,\sin ^{3}(2\theta)\,\sin ^{3}(2^{2}\theta)\cdots \sin ^{3}(2^{n-1}\theta)\Bigr] \;\sin (2^{n}\theta).

(a)  Put f(\theta)=\sin ^{2}\theta\,\sin (2\theta).  Show that f attains its maximum value 3\sqrt 3\,/\,8 and that this maximum is reached precisely for
        \theta \equiv \pi/3 \text{ or } \theta \equiv 4\pi/3 \pmod {2\pi}.

(b)  Prove that for every integer n\ge 1 and every real \theta
            |P_{n}(\theta)|\;\le \;(3\sqrt 3/8)^{\,n},
     and determine all \theta for which equality holds.

(c)  Deduce that for every real \theta and every integer n\ge 0
            \sin ^{2}\theta\;\sin ^{2}(2\theta)\;\sin ^{2}(2^{2}\theta)\;\cdots\;\sin ^{2}(2^{n}\theta)\;\le\;(3/4)^{\,n}.
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout write  M:=3\sqrt 3/8.

-------------------------------------------------
(a)  The maximum of f(\theta)=\sin ^{2}\theta\,\sin (2\theta).

Use the identity  \sin 2\theta=2\sin \theta\cos \theta:
     f(\theta)=2\sin ^{3}\theta\cos \theta.

Differentiate:
     f'(\theta)=2(3\sin ^{2}\theta\cos ^{2}\theta-\sin ^{4}\theta)
               =2\sin ^{2}\theta\,(3\cos ^{2}\theta-\sin ^{2}\theta)
               =2\sin ^{2}\theta\,(3-4\sin ^{2}\theta).

Thus f'(\theta)=0 when \sin \theta=0   (\theta=0,\pi ,2\pi) or when \sin ^{2}\theta=3/4 (\theta=\pi/3,2\pi/3,4\pi/3,5\pi/3).

Evaluating f at these critical points gives
     f(0)=f(\pi)=f(2\pi)=0,
     f(\pi/3)=f(4\pi/3)=+M,
     f(2\pi/3)=f(5\pi/3)=-M.
Because |f(\theta)|\le 2 for all \theta, the value M is the global maximum, reached exactly when \theta\equiv\pi/3 or 4\pi/3 (mod 2\pi).

-------------------------------------------------
(b)  Bounding |P_{n}(\theta)| and the equality cases.

Write
     P_{n}(\theta)=\sin ^{2}\theta\;\prod _{k=1}^{n-1}\bigl[\sin ^{2}(2^{k}\theta)\,\sin (2^{k}\theta)\bigr] \;\sin (2^{n}\theta)
                  =\prod _{j=0}^{n-1} f\bigl(2^{j}\theta\bigr).               (\*)

Taking absolute values and using part (a),
     |P_{n}(\theta)|\;=\;\prod _{j=0}^{n-1}|f(2^{j}\theta)|\;\le\;M^{n}.      (1)

Equality in (1) requires |f(2^{j}\theta)|=M for every j=0,1,\dots ,n-1; equivalently
                   2^{j}\theta\equiv \pi/3,\;2\pi/3,\;4\pi/3,\;5\pi/3 \pmod{2\pi}.      (2)
Because multiplication by 2 modulo \pi simply interchanges the two residue classes \pi/3 and 2\pi/3, condition (2) is satisfied for every j iff
                   \theta\equiv \pi/3 \text{ or } 2\pi/3 \pmod{\pi}.               (3)
Conversely, (3) indeed forces (2).  Hence
     |P_{n}(\theta)|=M^{n}\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad\theta=\pi/3+k\pi\text{ or }\theta=2\pi/3+k\pi\;(k\in\mathbb Z).
(The sign of P_{n}(\theta) may vary, but its magnitude is M^{n}.)

-------------------------------------------------
(c)  Bounding the product of even powers.

Put  A_{k}:=\sin ^{2}(2^{k}\theta)\;(0\le k\le n).  From (\*) one has
     |P_{n}|=A_{0}^{1}\,A_{1}^{3/2}\,A_{2}^{3/2}\cdots A_{n-1}^{3/2}\,A_{n}^{1/2}.
Taking the power 2/3 gives
     |P_{n}|^{2/3}=A_{0}^{2/3}\,A_{1}\,A_{2}\cdots A_{n-1}\,A_{n}^{1/3}.      (4)
Now
     Q_{n}:=\prod_{k=0}^{n}A_{k}=A_{0}\,A_{1}\cdots A_{n-1}\,A_{n}
          =\bigl(|P_{n}|^{2/3}\bigr)\;A_{0}^{1/3}\,A_{n}^{2/3}.                 (5)
Since 0\le A_{k}\le 1, the extra factors in (5) satisfy A_{0}^{1/3}A_{n}^{2/3}\le 1, whence
     Q_{n}\le |P_{n}|^{2/3}.                                                   (6)
Using (1), we obtain
     Q_{n}\le \bigl(M^{n}\bigr)^{2/3}=(M^{2/3})^{n}.
A direct calculation gives M^{2/3}=(3\sqrt 3/8)^{2/3}=3/4, so
     \boxed{\;\sin ^{2}\theta\,\sin ^{2}(2\theta)\,\cdots\,\sin ^{2}(2^{n}\theta)\le (3/4)^{\,n}\;}.  

Equality in (6) would require A_{0}=A_{n}=1, which is impossible when n\ge 1 because A_{k}=\sin ^{2}(\cdot)\le 1 and the equality |f(\cdot)|=M forces A_{k}=3/4.  Hence the inequality in part (c) is strict for every n\ge 1.

The problem is completely solved.

core_steps: Maximize f(θ)=sin^2θ·sin(2θ) on [0,2π] via one–variable calculus (gives θ*=π/3 mod π).
Induct: because 2^nθ* attains f’s maximum for every n, E_n(θ) is maximized at θ*.
Evaluate E_n(θ*) to obtain exact maximum, take 2/3-power, and enlarge the end exponents to deduce ∏_{k=0}^{n}sin^2(2^kθ) ≤ (3/4)^n.

slot1.description: Exponent on the leading sine factor and, consequently, (exponent+1) on each interior factor; changing 2→a (with a>0) and 3→a+1 leaves ratio E_{n+1}/E_n = sin^a x·sin(2x) unchanged in form and the whole proof goes through verbatim.

slot2.description: Specific maximizer θ*=π/3 (and 4π/3); any value that maximizes sin^aθ·sin(2θ) and is carried by doubling into the same maximizing set for all powers would serve.

slot3.description: Numerical bound (3/4)^n that appears after evaluating at θ*; this constant changes automatically if slot1 or slot2 is altered, but the inequality’s structure stays identical.
